claims.text: Professional investors (VCs and institutional capital) only generate returns on companies that reach multi-billion dollar valuations; as a result, they evaluate every investment by counting how many assumptions must be true simultaneously for the business to reach that scale, and they bid the valuation higher as the assumption count decreases.
conditions: Business targeting a $1B+ ultimate valuation
Institutional venture capital context
claims.mechanism: VC fund math requires billion-dollar exits to return the fund; a company with fewer unproved assumptions has higher expected value at a given stage because each remaining assumption is a multiplicative discount on the exit probability.
bounded_by: Does not apply to businesses targeting sub-$100M exits
Does not describe angel or strategic investors whose return expectations differ from institutional VC

claims.text: When a capital constraint (not an assumption constraint) is the last remaining blocker to scale, the valuation step-up is defensible at a much higher multiple because the investor is no longer buying uncertainty about whether the model works; they are buying execution with known economics.
conditions: All major business model assumptions (technology, market, regulatory, operational) have been validated
The only remaining blocker is access to manufacturing, distribution, or operational capital
claims.mechanism: Investors price risk; when risk is reduced to a single-factor capital problem rather than multi-factor assumption uncertainty, expected value of the investment rises and justifies a premium valuation.
bounded_by: Does not apply if regulatory or technology risk remains material
Requires credible evidence that all prior assumptions are genuinely proved, not just asserted

claims.text: In a fundraising pitch, founders should lead with story over facts because investors are not buying science or technology; they are buying a narrative about the likelihood of making money, and facts are only useful insofar as they support a compelling story.
conditions: Biotech or highly technical sectors where the audience cannot evaluate the science independently
Pitching to non-specialist capital (athletes, celebrities, financial investors without domain expertise)
claims.mechanism: Non-specialist investors cannot adjudicate technical claims; their decision is based on narrative confidence and personal trust in the founder, not scientific merit.
bounded_by: Does not apply to scientific grant applications or deep-tech specialist investors who require technical diligence
Does not apply when the investor pool includes domain experts who will evaluate the underlying science

claims.text: The core investor question a founder must answer before setting a fundraising valuation is: what risk did investors at the prior round take on, and what tangible evidence now proves that risk has been resolved? The valuation step-up is the numerical reward for that de-risking.
conditions: Series A to B or later-stage follow-on rounds
Valuation step-up of 3x or more being requested
claims.mechanism: Investors who came in at a lower valuation need a logical reason to accept a higher price; that reason is the reduction in risk they took on that earlier investors absorbed.

claims.text: Raising capital with total dilution of roughly 35% of equity (founder retaining 65%) leaves a founder in a defensible control position, provided voting rights are structured favorably, even when going out for a large subsequent round that would dilute equity further.
conditions: Founder retains at least 50-65% ownership prior to the next round
Voting rights are structured to give founder majority vote
claims.mechanism: At 65% economic ownership with standard or favorable voting rights, a founder retains board and decision-making control even after a subsequent 20% dilution round, leaving them with roughly 45-50% economic ownership and majority votes.

numbers.axis: Founder equity ownership percentage across funding stages

bounded_by: Does not apply if a future round requires selling more than 30-40% of the company
Does not apply if investors negotiate for board seats that override voting rights

claims.text: To prepare a large fundraising round, a founder should have early conversations with a large pool of potential investors (approximately 10x the target number) to map what each one needs to believe in order to invest, then filter out unrealistic demands and focus on the subset of needs-to-believe that are both achievable and affordable to demonstrate.
conditions: Planning a round 12-24 months in advance
Targeting institutional or high-net-worth investors
claims.mechanism: Pre-mapping investor requirements identifies the lowest-cost de-risking actions that unlock the most capital; filtering out outlier demands avoids wasting resources on conditions that will never be satisfied.

numbers.axis: Investor pipeline size relative to target close count

anchors.value: 10x the number of investors you plan to close
the filtered subset whose needs are achievable

bounded_by: Assumes sufficient time horizon (12+ months) to conduct discovery before the round closes
Does not apply to emergency bridge rounds where speed overrides preparation

claims.text: In a high-valuation fundraising pitch, the founder's personal credibility (a sale on the person) is as important as the business story (a sale on the narrative); investors in non-expert capital pools are primarily betting on the founder's judgment and trustworthiness, not the technical merit of the business.
conditions: Investors without domain expertise in the founder's industry (e.g., professional athletes, celebrities, general-wealth investors)
Large round size relative to investor's prior experience with the sector
claims.mechanism: Non-specialist investors cannot verify technical claims, so trust in the founder becomes the primary risk-reduction mechanism replacing technical due diligence.
bounded_by: Does not apply to institutional VCs with sector expertise who perform independent technical due diligence
Does not apply to regulated investment contexts (e.g., public markets) where prospectus disclosures dominate

claims.text: Alex Hormozi argues that detailed planning before execution is overrated for entrepreneurs because no plan survives first contact with reality — the plan will change the moment actual work begins, so obsessing over it delays the feedback that only action can provide.
conditions: early-stage entrepreneurs launching a new product, service, or business
claims.mechanism: Plans are invalidated on first contact with actual customers and conditions; the information needed to make a good plan only becomes available through execution, so over-planning delays the learning loop without improving the plan.

claims.text: Alex Hormozi asserts that the primary success variable observable among high-income earners and high achievers is the extent to which they execute — not the quality or detail of their plan — and that this becomes obvious when spending time in mastermind circles of people who are doing well.
conditions: peer observation across high-income operator circles

claims.text: Alex Hormozi claims that when launching a new service or product, the fastest path is to find a customer first, sell them on the promise that you will help them in some meaningful way and will not leave until they are satisfied, and then figure out the delivery — rather than building a product first and then finding buyers.
conditions: entrepreneurs trying to launch a new service or product
no existing proven delivery system yet

claims.text: Alex Hormozi asserts that iteration through doing — not planning — reveals what works: he changed his job or business model six times before figuring out what was working, and he would not have discovered it without actually doing the work with real customers.
conditions: early-career or early-stage entrepreneurial phase
claims.mechanism: Real-world execution provides feedback that no amount of planning can simulate; each attempt eliminates bad paths and surfaces the one that works.

numbers.axis: number of job or model changes before finding what worked
